methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Anyon Systems is a Series A quantum computing company that has raised C$23M+ to date. The quantum computing sector comprises a modest but meaningful peer universe: 3 public companies (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private firms including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

The peer-median approach is not applicable here. The narrow peer set—filtered by stage, proximity, and valuation tier—yields fewer than the three datapoints required for statistical validity. While 7 same-sector peers exist, none satisfy simultaneous same-stage and similar-tier constraints. An all-sector median would introduce unacceptable noise given the heterogeneity within quantum computing (ion traps vs. superconducting vs. photonic modalities, different commercialization timelines, and development maturity levels). Triangulation via comparable company analysis is therefore limited by data scarcity at the Series A stage in this nascent sector.

The lack of a robust peer-based valuation floor reflects a structural gap: quantum computing lacks sufficient public or early-stage private transactions to anchor conventional multiples. Any valuation would require alternative methods—DCF analysis of technical milestones, venture capital method applied to exit scenarios, or pricing relative to capital deployed—none of which are supported by the provided data. Key uncertainties include the target's specific technical differentiation, IP position, and revenue trajectory relative to the broader peer set.
